steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: What is the energy source that keeps a main sequence star stable?
options: Nuclear fusion of hydrogen nuclei into helium
Nuclear fission of uranium deep in the star’s core
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Stars are powered by nuclear fusion, not combustion or fission. Hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium, releasing enormous energy. This outward radiation pressure balances gravity, keeping the star stable.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Main sequence stars are powered by nuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium. The energy released as outward pressure balances the inward pull of gravity.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.8.2 — Life cycle of stars

steps.title: Life Cycle of Stars
steps.bodyHTML: Stars form from a cloud of gas and dust called a nebula, which contracts under gravity to form a protostar and then a stable main sequence star powered by nuclear fusion. A star the size of our Sun eventually swells into a red giant, then sheds its outer layers to leave a white dwarf; more massive stars become red supergiants and end in a supernova explosion, leaving either a neutron star or, for the most massive stars, a black hole.
commonErrors: Thinking the Sun will explode as a supernova — it is not massive enough; it will become a red giant then white dwarf
Confusing the order: protostar comes before main sequence, not after
Forgetting that a supernova is the explosion, not the star itself

aqa.questionText: Describe the life cycle of a star much more massive than the Sun, starting from the main sequence stage.

steps.label: Main sequence to red supergiant
steps.content: When hydrogen fuel runs low, the core contracts and heats, causing the outer layers to expand. The star becomes a red supergiant.

steps.label: Supernova
steps.content: The star undergoes a catastrophic explosion called a supernova, releasing enormous energy and dispersing heavy elements into space.

steps.label: Neutron star or black hole
steps.content: The remnant core collapses. For very massive stars, it becomes a black hole. For moderately massive stars, it becomes a neutron star.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — neutron star; 1 mark — black hole (for most massive)

aqa.finalAnswer: Main sequence → red supergiant → supernova → neutron star (or black hole for most massive).

aqa.questionText: What will eventually happen to our Sun at the end of its main sequence stage?

options.label: A
options.text: It will explode as a supernova and leave a neutron star

options.label: B
options.text: It will collapse directly into a black hole

options.label: C
options.text: It will expand into a red giant then cool to a white dwarf

options.label: D
options.text: It will shrink into a protostar and begin the cycle again

markSchemeSteps: The Sun is not massive enough for a supernova — it will expand to a red giant, then shed its layers leaving a white dwarf ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option A is the path for much more massive stars only — the Sun is an average-mass star.
